**Timothy Andrew Fischer AC FTSE** (3 May 1946 â 22 August 2019) was an Australian politician and diplomat who served as leader of the National Party of Australia from 1990 to 1999 and as the 10th Deputy Prime Minister of Australia from 1996 to 1999 under Prime Minister John Howard.

---

### Early Life and Military Service

Fischer was born on 3 May 1946 in Lockhart, New South Wales, the fourth of five children. He grew up on the family property at Boree Creek and attended Boree Creek Public School before boarding at Xavier College in Melbourne from 1958, graduating in 1963.

In 1966, Fischer was conscripted into the Australian Army and commissioned at the Officer Training Unit, Scheyville. He served as a Second Lieutenant with the 1st Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment (1RAR) from July 1966 to March 1969, including service in the Vietnam War. He was wounded during the Battle of Coral-Balmoral in MayâJune 1968. Upon his return, he took over the family farming property at Boree Creek.

### State Politics

Fischer entered politics as a member of the Country Party (later National Party). He served in the New South Wales Legislative Assembly representing the seat of Sturt from 1971 to 1980 and then Murray from 1980 to 1984. He served on the opposition frontbench from 1978 to 1984.

### Federal Politics

In 1984, Fischer won the federal seat of Farrer for the National Party. He quickly rose through the ranks, known for his rustic manner that concealed a shrewd political mind. In 1990, he succeeded Charles Blunt as leader of the National Party, defeating former leader Ian Sinclair.

When the Howard government was elected in 1996, Fischer became **Deputy Prime Minister** and **Minister for Trade**. Key moments of his tenure include:
- Strongly supporting tough **gun control measures** after the 1996 Port Arthur massacre, despite opposition from his rural base
- Counter-attacking the rise of **Pauline Hanson's One Nation** party during the 1998 election campaign
- Navigating tensions over free-market reforms affecting rural industries
- Advocating for the extinguishment of native title rights following the Mabo and Wik decisions

He was affectionately nicknamed **"Two-Minute Tim"** for his hectic schedule of brief visits to rural branch meetings.

In 1999, Fischer surprised colleagues by resigning as party leader and minister, announcing his retirement from politics (effective 2001). His decision was partly motivated by family demands, particularly caring for his son Harrison, who has autism. Fischer himself claimed to have "high functioning" autism, though he was never professionally diagnosed.

### Post-Political Career

After retiring from politics, Fischer remained highly active:
- **Chairman of Tourism Australia** (2004â2007)
- **Chairman of the Crawford Fund** (2001â2006), supporting international agricultural research
- **Vice-chair and Chair of the Crop Trust** (2013â2017) and a vigorous supporter of the Svalbard Global Seed Vault
- **National Chairman of the Royal Flying Doctor Service**
- **Founding Patron of Australia for UNHCR** (2001â2006)
- **Australia's first resident Ambassador to the Holy See** (2009â2012), nominated by PM Kevin Rudd

He was appointed a **Companion of the Order of Australia (AC)** in the 2005 Australia Day Honours.

### Personal Life and Passions

Fischer married Judy Brewer in 1992; they had two sons. He was famously Australia's best-known **rail enthusiast**, having studied rail gauges as a childhood hobby.
